insights: Charlie Whitherspoon handles 59% of Critical+High priority tickets—13 percentage points above Fred Luddy’s 46%—making priority concentration the primary driver of managerial workload imbalance in system user administration.

Despite perfectly balanced ticket volume (100 tickets each), Charlie Whitherspoon carries the highest composite management overload risk (score 95.03), directly attributable to disproportionate exposure to high-criticality work rather than quantity or backlog.

Hardware and Inquiry/Help categories exhibit the highest Critical+High rates (53% each), while Software has the lowest (44%), indicating category-specific risk amplification that compounds Charlie Whitherspoon’s exposure given his higher share of these high-priority categories.

Charlie Whitherspoon manages 34% of his tickets from the United States—nearly triple the 12–20% range of other assignees—suggesting geographic concentration may reinforce priority-based overload through localized demand complexity.

No temporal clustering or open backlog exists to compound overload risk: all assignees show mild monthly workload variation (peak 11–14%), and all 500 tickets are Closed/Resolved, confirming that current imbalance is structural—not situational or operational.

The 50% incidence of negative resolution times signals systemic timestamp inconsistency, but this data quality issue does not affect priority- or category-based imbalance findings, which remain robust and actionable.
summary: The evaluation reveals no volume-based imbalance in managerial role distribution—each of the five IT managers handles exactly 100 tickets—but a pronounced structural imbalance driven by priority concentration: Charlie Whitherspoon bears 59% Critical+High workload, significantly exceeding the team’s 46–50% range and earning the highest composite overload risk score (95.03). This disparity is amplified by his elevated share of high-risk categories (Hardware, Inquiry/Help) and US-based tickets, while temporal patterns and closed-state completeness confirm the issue is not transient or backlog-driven. The root cause is therefore assignment policy misalignment—not capacity shortage—making priority-weighted redistribution the most direct lever for mitigating management overload in system user administration. Next focus should be auditing assignment rules for priority-category-location coupling and piloting priority-adjusted ticket routing to rebalance criticality exposure.
